Light: For once, I’ve stolen all the breath from your lips as I always dreamed. Please, look after Sophia for me. Fix what humanity broke, once and for all.

The elevator dings as Madeline Light releases the hold door button, and the doors close. Director Moose, now O5-07, is alone with SCP-001.

Several minutes of silence.

Director Moose: That was—

SCP-001: Shh. I’m not ready to talk yet.

SCP-001 wipes its eyes with its dress sleeve. After several more minutes of silence, Moose speaks again.

Director Moose: So, are you going to—

SCP-001: I told her my true name, and she learned that I was not born in the shape I now claim.

Director Moose sips from her glass.

Director Moose: Ah. That explains that.

SCP-001: That explains that.

Director Moose: Mhmm. That’s what I just said.

Several seconds of silence.

SCP-001: I marvel at your ability to remain nonchalant in such moments.

Director Moose: There’s always time for gravitas, and time for humor as well.

SCP-001: The greatest trick a magician has, timing.

Director Moose smiles. Several seconds of silence pass.

Director Moose: Well, here we are, finally alone again.

SCP-001: Here we are.

SCP-001 and Director Moose look out the windows at Eurtec.

Director Moose: Do you ever get fatigued, thinking about the momentum, the monumental cliff that we face?

SCP-001: Some days it’s harder to shake these ancient bones from the bed. But we get up. We go on because that is what it requires, that is the sacrifice necessary to save the grand tree.

Director Moose: Do you ever think you’ve sacrificed enough for one life? You, of all people, especially.

SCP-001: Read the files, have we?

Director Moose: But not that book. Knowing the name is enough, enough to know what you’ve been through—and yet…

SCP-001: And yet you only know pieces. And yet She still sings in the deep, waiting. The hour hand moves ever closer, the ticking ever louder.

Director Moose: Being around those two, I think I’ve started to hear it.

SCP-001: They don’t hear it yet. Neither do the others, The Twins, The Queen in her gardens of the dead, The Witch on her island in the depths, and so many others. I’ve never stopped hearing it, but it’s getting louder.

Director Moose: It’s getting closer then?

SCP-001: Four rings of the rock around Helios until the Sapphire and the return of the engineer. Three until the return of the blade. Fourteen until the grand palace rises anew into the sky, and the seals fail. She stirs in the deep. I can feel Her waking.

Director Moose: So, this is it then, this is the one.

SCP-001 exhales heavily.

SCP-001: This is the one.

Several seconds of silence.

Director Moose: Was the plan all along to have the Foundation fight them?

SCP-001: Yes.

Director Moose: Fighting reality itself. It sounds—

SCP-001: Absurd? Insane?

Director Moose: Yes.

SCP-001: If it was all of them at the same time? Yes.

Director Moose: Wait, what do you mean?

SCP-001: Come now Moose, you are more clever than that.

Director Moose: I’m serious this time.

SCP-001: Must I spell everything out?

Director Moose: Like I’m ten.

SCP-001 scowls.

SCP-001: Everything has been a delaying action.

Moose looks at SCP-001 opening her mouth slightly and holding it there for several seconds.

Director Moose: What?

SCP-001: A. Delaying. Action. Enough stabilization to space out the chain. It will be as it was then. One at a time.

Director Moose stares out the window for several seconds. Her jaw clenches and unclenches.

Director Moose: Holy fuck.

SCP-001: It is the only way we have a chance. Everything else has been manipulation to increase the odds. No more than a decade and a half away, then we go to war. Then, then we shall fight once again with the Old Gods of the world. A war in heaven.

Approximately one minute of silence.

Director Moose: How many times, how many times have you done this to find the perfect conditions?

SCP-001: Too many. Trillions dead. The cost was high in those threads. It will still be high now in this one. Maybe another iteration can improve the odds if we fail. Maybe even if we succeed.

Several minutes of silence.

SCP-001: Moose?

Director Moose: Yes, Seeress?

SCP-001: I am afraid.

Director Moose: I know. There are still many years left to go, and many more dice to roll. But I am also afraid of what lies at the end. Especially with what you’ve just told me.

Several seconds of silence. Director Moose looks out the window.

Director Moose: There is a lot of work to do, child of Ilion. A lot of work to return the Singers to the fold.

SCP-001: There always is.

SCP-001 and Director Moose stare out the window at Eurtec below.

SCP-001: The days ahead are greatly muddied by Her reawakening. Yet curiously, I do not know when the next avatar of primordial fire will appear.

Director Moose: The what? You what?

SCP-001: Idle musings of a deranged old lady, pay them no mind.

Director Moose: C—

SCP-001: Not while they are recording.

<End Log>










EPILOGUE


AWAKENING


MY NATURE IN THE THREADS















Addendum 26: SCP-001 Journal J-001

On May 15th, 2021, O5-07 Madeline Light reported to the amnestics clinic in Eurtec for debriefing and memory erasure as part of standard retirement protocols. During debriefing, she surrendered the only sensitive item remaining on her person, a thick leatherbound book containing SCP-001’s personal journal. The following document contains the most relevant excerpts linked to identifying SCP-001 based on early life events that were previously undisclosed. These chapters were the only ones accessible to Foundation staff; the remaining pages contain an ontokinetic defense mechanism which prevents viewing beyond a certain point through unknown means.

Foreword: I sit here at the top of this sacred place among a field of blooming flowers in the spring of the year 4957 from when the seas were set in earth and sky. Here I have elected, in the event that things do not work out as I have foreseen, to paint for you, whoever may be reading this tome, whether it be in the Library or some other misbegotten place, the story of how I came to be.

Even now as I sit here staring at the sunrise, I can see the grand canvas of history sprawl out before me in a stream of rippling color.

Yes, see.

This is the story of my birth, the story of how someone who was not me died, and the weave of my life was forged and sewn together.

Chapter 1: On A Life Recorded in the Annals of Myth

She, that girl who seems so very far away now, was born in a palace beneath the glimmering light of Helios’s sun. A place of sweet and bitter scents in perpetual contradiction. It sat atop a lush hill, overlooking a fertile coastal plain on the edge of an empire whose name is long forgotten. Tall walls, a merry life of people used to the ways of war found in the south and east.

She was born on a warm summer evening, when the smell of dew was unusually strong on the wind and some ancient chthonic intent had crept up from beneath the earth to burrow into newborn flesh. The elder of fraternal twins, she was loved. Born to a king and queen beneath a greater mantle who stared down at her glimmering eyes with an adoration and gentle warmth that was not built to last.

They named her in the old ways of the world, but it was not her true name. There was interference from the Scarlet and the Maker of Chains. She was not born a girl as she should have been, and the world was worse for it. And though they sat there adoring their new tiny bundles, those ill-fated parents did not yet realize what power they had brought into the world.

Summers began to flow together in the endless paints streaming across the sky.

She grew.

She took her first steps beneath the all-knowing sun and found her footing in the world amidst the rolling grasses and lush forests.

She saw things that others didn’t, couldn’t, but didn’t yet know what they meant. Heard muffled choirs in a rolling pitch, washed with aromas to which not even her parents would have been exposed.

They were shadows dancing in dreams unspoken.

And with every step, every inch gained, fundamental incongruence with the world grew.

And with every step, every inch gained, a roiling black dissonance seeped into flesh and nerve.

Every glance into the clear fountains of the palace, those mirrored surfaces of shining bronze were a reminder that she was wrong. This flesh that was hers was not hers, but a stranger’s.

A wrongness that she could not escape.

The smiles and laughs in play became fewer with every passing summer. Discord in flesh and soul plunging what should have been a happy soul into the dour abyss above a hungry underworld.

The discomfort of that ill-fitting skin did not go unnoticed by the world.

It was seen by opportunists looking for diamonds in the rough.

When she was eight summers tall, she and her brother came to rest in a grand marble colored space in the palace. It glimmered with golden statues of a radiant young man bearing in his arms a lyre and upon his back and glimmering bow. It reeked of incense and herbs so strong that it made her eyes water. Brother of the Hunter, born of prophecy, medicine, music and inheritor of a chariot of fire across the sky. They slept there beneath the swirling color and twinkling lullabies when they grew too tired to continue play.

In a waking dream this opportunist came, skin glimmering with twinkles of sunlight and backswept hair framing blue eyes.

“Children of Ilion, I have seen you this night aslumber at my hallowed feet.” His voice was deceptively honeyed to her ears.

He was awash in scents of baked breads and cream filled things that she could not grasp.

She stared up in wonder for him, for the threads of those above seldom graced the dreams of mortals.

“Your grace, what honor you bestow on us,” she said, bowing at his feet.

He smiled. She believed it warm, welcoming, friendly and filled with favor.

How could she have known how wrong she was?

“I have foreseen your greatness in the twirling stars above. I come before you because you will make great marks upon history and the weave of destiny, and I seek to aid you in that quest in return for favor owed.”

His eyes turned to her, shimmering in the midnight starlight.

“My lord, you bless us with your presence and gifts? Name thy favor and we will meet it!” she cried, not understanding the price that would one day be paid.

So great was this moment it blinded her growing gifts to the truth of his desire. The lust in his soul that would only grow.

“Worry not about the favor requested now, you are too young to fulfill such merits. To you,” he gestured to her brother. “The great gift of prophecy I will bestow. And to your sister—”

She gasped, tears springing to her eyes at such novel validation. He saw her for what she was. What she should be. He looked at her sending chills up her spine from the intensity of his otherworldly gaze.

What she missed was the momentary furrowing of brows and confusion dancing in his eyes.

What had he seen?

It didn’t matter for the look was gone in the flash of a breath.

“To you, the gift of prescience, the grandest human prophet to ever live you will be. But more than that I will correct the wrong in your flesh.”

She gasped for air in disbelief. What dreams she had not realized needed to be fulfilled, and now they were upon her. She wept in relief for this greatest of mercies.

How could she have known that prophecy was already hers?

How could she have known the true cost of the shifting of her flesh?

“Will you take these great wonders I offer you and adorn yourself in my favor?” he cooed.

“Yes,” they said together.

How could they refuse?

He stepped forward and placed that shimmering skin against their heads. Their eyes burned with deep and heavenly fire. That little girl’s flesh writhed against her bones, shape changing and flourishing in bursts of glittery flame.

With a great rush of air and plunging stomachs, as if falling through an eternal starlit sky, they awoke in the opportunist’s temple. Two serpents lapped their tongues in their ears, entwined in copulation. They slithered away when they, the young girl and her brother, sat up.

The prince looked upon the princess and cried out her name.

“You are a girl!”

She looked upon herself, the beautiful bundles of her hair, and the reflections in the nearest pool and polished metal and felt congruence. She burst into happy tears.

“I am a girl!” And she was truly in all ways.

The King and Queen were brought hastily to the temple and saw what had become of their children. They praised on high the truth of otherworldly visitation, proclaiming their child a daughter, a princess, a prophet to be.

So, they brought great fortune tellers from across their kingdom to instruct their children in the art of prophecy, though they truly did not need it. Their gifts were so potent as to eclipse their teachers within a few months of lessons.

Her brother grew and grew into a grand Augur with every passing year, but none could match her strength of sight, not even him. She flourished in her new skin, renewed with life and happiness, no longer trapped by Misery’s chthonic grasps.

She saw everything that could be, everything that might be, everything that had been, and everything that would never be. She was still so young, and the art of determining which belonged to which yet remained to be mastered.

At fifteen summers, her parents sought a proper husband, a prince from some far-off land to secure alliances.

But she was never interested in the idle looks and politics of men.

She preferred the sweet scents of wives, the gentle touches of feminine skin and the callouses of strong huntresses.

She flirted with the women of the huntress’s temple and became a priest of two, the opportunist and the wise owl in her shielded armor. In part fulfilling foreseen prophecy, in part to avoid the forced irritations of an unhappy marriage.

The king and queen had many children and did not despair their daughter’s choices. They were, in their own ways, good parents to her.

But their youngest they spoiled, a beautiful little boy who had grown into a heartthrob all the same.

On a cool autumn evening when she was sixteen summers tall, a dreadful wind blew through the princess’s chambers.

The opportunist had come for his promised favor.

He appeared in a burst of glittering moonlight whilst she dozed away in her bed. He sat on the edge of her postered repose, and drug warm fingers across her cheeks, stirring her from slumber. He smiled lazily at her, as if his intent was perfectly clear.

He pulled away the sheets, pressing his hand to her chest to keep her from fully rising.

“Lord, what—how may I serve you this night?” she asked him, shrinking in that predatory glimmer filling his eyes.

“I have come for my favor, sweet priestess.”

Her stomach twisted in a nauseous knot.

“What might I do for you?”

Without hesitation he spoke of his desire.

“I would make you mine. I would lie with you, make sweetest love with you now until eternity. Marry you underneath the glimmering sun.”

The whole world turned on its side as she realized the choice before her, as his hands caressed her thighs. Bile filled her throat, the scene of rotting flesh sharp against her nostrils. She could not bear it.

“No.”

He stopped, hand firmly grasping her thigh. Too firmly for it began to hurt, and tears pooled in the corners of her eyes. He stared down at her, as she trembled against the bed.

“What did you say?”

“No, I don’t want to marry. I don’t want to lie with you.”

He released her thigh, constricted blood painfully throbbing through the limb. He pressed his hand against her chest harshly while leaning forward, constricting her breath. He roared, his voice a layered boom that filled all the room and his breath stank of rotting meat.

“YOU WOULD DENY ME?”

“I will not lie with someone I do not love. I will not marry someone who I do not love!” She panicked, squirming against his grasp.

Her heart hammered in her chest, feeling the hot sting of heat burning from the sun he embodied against her chest.

“I made you! I gave you everything, fixed you when you were broken, gave you sight when you could not see. You dare to refuse my love?”

What could she say to soothe this angered spirit other than the truth in all its poetic aspiration?

“I could never love you like you deserve. I could not live with you in every moment you craved, cradle you in the warm morning sun in a grassy field, argue over the little things in the arrangement of paintings on the wall, the cleanliness of plates in the cupboard. I could not be the mother to your children; I could not be the mortal woman you seek in your heart. I do not love men; you seek something I could not give!” she cried out in fear.

“You ungrateful whore!” he screamed in her face, spittle splattering and sizzling against her skin. “Upon you I cast the greatest of curses. From this day forward with these eyes that tempt me you shall never see the world as it is again.”

She cried out in agony as his worldly influence poisoned her vision with stinging revulsion. Black swallowed everything in the empty void of Chaos’s reign. Blood dripped from the corners of her eyes, staining her white gown.

“I doom you to never again be believed, your prophecies will ring true for yourself, but your words will be deafened and absurd to those who you most want to believe them.”

She wept in the abyss of sightlessness feeling the curse take effect, crawling beneath her skin in sunkissed marks. Then, flesh shifted in a place that was most sensitive and she screamed. She prayed and prayed and prayed to the owl on high.

“Finally, I take back what I once ga—” but before his magic could writhe again through her flesh, the medallion of the owl burned with otherworldly vigor and scalded his hand, blocking the fullness of the curse.

“Interference!” he screeched as shadow fell on the room. The cry of an owl echoed and he retreated. Then he was gone in a puff of light.

But the damage was done. He had undone a piece of her flesh, stolen her sight, and left her destitute and doomed to be disbelieved.

And her brother?

Her brother was left unmarred, having gladly accepted the patron’s favor.

The twin brother looked upon her with pity, and though he at first tried to support her, defend her visions, she was poison.

Even the eldest brother, long gentle and soft with the sister he so loved, could not defend her.

So began the slow spiral of madness that would doom her family to its end. The rumors curled throughout the city that the once great oracle of the east had fallen into madness, insanity. Her visions, predictions of wealth or ruin were ignored. Every prediction said aloud resulted in a choice that would ensure the worst would come to be.

It only got worse.

Having seen twenty summers, she foresaw a cataclysm, the end of her parents’ kingdom, the end of her people by the choice of her youngest brother.

Influence from on high would come to him with a choice, a gift of vanity and flattery or a gift of wisdom. Saw him leave for a foreign fortress where he would doom them all.

She begged him not to listen at a table filled with the aroma of delicious meats and sweet fruits. She begged him to refuse to play the game in the twilight hours of the morning as the first kisses of the sun were trying to crest the horizon.

“If you go, you will ruin us all!”

“You wish for suffering and misery in this house of our blood. You lost yourself in madness and now you want to drag us all with you. Do us all the good of shutting up and throwing yourself into the sea.”

He didn’t listen.

He insulted her, demeaned and degraded.

Had her dragged off to her room where she was locked in for three days.

He went. Left on a boat as she stood in her window, watching with sightless eyes as he rode into a bleeding sunset.

She foresaw his return, with a beautiful woman, a foreign queen in a golden veil. Her people rejoiced, they sang, burned bonfires that smote the air with ash and pleasant pheromonic woods. They sacrificed animals to the gods above, filling the plaza with the metallic iron of blood. They welcomed him with open arms, oblivious to the heralding of their doom.

She stood and watched through sight and sightless eyes as they ascended the steps of the palace.

She was inconsolable with rage and despair.

She leapt out of the crowd, pouncing upon the woman and tearing at her hair and that golden veil.

“You’ve doomed us all! Everything will burn, blood will fill the river and bay, and nothing will be left!”

She was pulled away by the guards, dragged screaming about death and the end to her room in the palace where she was locked inside for three days and nights. She lay curled up on the bed and wept for the state of things. Cried to the opportunist.

“God! Oh God of Prophecy!
Thou hast ruined me. Thou has doomed me.”

The only person who dared to visit the maddened oracle was the eldest brother. A man among men, always gentle with her, and even more so now. He listened, even though he did not believe.

But for a moment, it felt as if someone believed her.

The ruin began with the arrival of those foreign kings, with grand armies of the greatest warriors told of in stories and minstrel’s tales from travelers abroad. The air stunk with their terrible musk and manly odors.

Great men by the thousands, so many of whom were doomed to die.

They demanded the return of their stolen Queen, but her youngest brother refused. Her parents, blinded by disbelief in her warnings of doom, foolishly supported him.

It was war.

A raging, hateful war that ballooned into the arrival of great flesh crafters of the northwest, the ancient blood and plant lovers of the east, the mechanized children of the northeast and west in their grand ticking gears and four legged monstrosities, and finally the singers of the sea from the far north come to aid the wronged kings.

It was there on those walls she saw the great evisceration of her people’s patrons at the hands of something much older. There, deep in the throes of war upon the palisades she first felt the warmth of Creation’s caress against her cheeks.

But it would not save them.

Any of them.

Her eldest brother was the first to fall. Speared through by a great warrior enraged by the death of a lover. She saw it long before it happened and wept for him. Begged him in the dim lights of fate not to leave.

But he did not believe her.

He refused to decline a challenge and rode forth on chariot to meet his end. It was a grisly end. She stood on the ramparts of the wall, the last light of sun’s warm kiss wafting against her skin and the smell of rotting death on the wind. She listened to the shouts of a victorious warrior and the sickening crunch of bone and flesh smashing against the ground.

Her brother, defiled in defeat, dragged across the battlefield by chariot.

She accosted the youngest brother then, screaming in his face.

“It’s your fault! This is all your fault! If only you had listened, he would have lived!”

He reeked of cowardice and failure. Too afraid to face what he had begun until she ripped apart his pride with increasing furor. Her youngest brother, aggrieved with blame, took a bow and shot dead that champion of the west.

But it was all so very little in the face of what was to come.

The greatest betrayal was the desertion of her twin brother.

That too she saw coming.

In the howling wind of a dreadful morning, she begged him not to carry through beneath the fetid stench of carts of bodies being hauled off to burn.

But like the rest, the curse had infected him too, and he went anyway.

A traitor to her people, whispering in their ears of all the secrets of their defense.

The doom was fully here and there was nothing she could do, though she would try.

The grand tactician, a man so hopelessly brilliant that none in her home could match him, derived the final death knell. A gift offering of peace left on the shores as their fleet mock sailed away. A grand wooden burden beast. A feast with endless drinks was declared as they rolled it in.

But she saw the truth. Of course she saw the truth. Dozens of men waiting inside for the guards and soldiers to get fat and drunk and fall asleep. They would pile out of the horse, open the gates, and that would be the very end.

Hysterical with rage when she was ignored by the King and Queen, she snatched an axe from a guard, and a torch from the wall, and sprinted at the gift. Screaming. The air was filled with the scent of wines, searing meat and cooking breads. The sound of snoring men, drunken soldiers, and giggling wives all around.

“They’re inside! They’re inside! They’re waiting inside to end us all!”

They stopped her, of course they stopped her.

“Poor princess, lost to madness and insanity. Blinded by the sight and unable to tell what’s real from what’s false. Even if they did put men in there, it would never be enough.”

She was dragged away, weeping. Unable to stop fate’s cruel march forward. They were going to lock her in her room again, but she couldn’t let that happen. There would be no coming back out.

So, she broke free, she fled to the house of wisdom, with its wooden statue of the armored strategist ever watching. Here she claimed sanctuary.

“Oh, spirit of Wisdom, grant this humble priestess your protection and asylum!”

She sat beneath the statue for hours, waiting. Waiting for it to come.

Come it did, with fire and blood and shouts of terror. The doors opening as women sought sanctuary, burying themselves deeper into the marble. She smelt ash, smoke, burning wood and flesh.

She begged the wise goddess for her protection again and again.

Then he burst into the room.

A savage man wearing animal skins, full of muscle. He strode across the floor and grabbed at her. She leapt forward, clinging to the statue, begging for help, for protection. So hard did he pull, the statue was toppled from its housing and cracked against the floor.

He reeked of musk, smoke and ash. His laugh filled her ears.

She died there. She died there where the last of her dignity, the last of her innocence was snuffed out against the backdrop of flames.

That’s where I was born. Weeping on the floor of the house of wisdom as he towered over me.

I saw the thunder come before it struck, the quaking steps of another entering the temple.

A great clap of light burning in the dark and a tremendous crack splitting the air.

He was knocked from his feet, sent tumbling across the floor. When he got back up, the skin of his shoulder sizzled the air with smoke and the flesh was an angry welt of red in the Sight.

There was a shadow of something tall in the backlit flames as troy burned in the distance. Something towering in the doorframe of that sacred space. Something I could not fully see.

“You dare come between me and my prize?!” In the Sight he stood there, staring with fury on his face, body half covered by his skins.

“This house is sacred to your kind,” a new voice said with a ferocity in its breath that I could barely comprehend.

“And? She is my conquest by right.”

There came no reply from the new voice. Only a sense of contained fury.

“Begone gilled princess and leave me my prize.”

“This ends by decree of your gods for this sacrilege. You will never accost another woman.”

There was bile in my throat. I could smell the ozone, the singed skin lingering in my nostrils.

Shouts echoed in the distance.

He roared, spear in one hand, shield in another as he banged the spear against it.

He was a fool. Even my people knew the reputation of the Singers of the Sea.

Her magic smote the air with great hateful flame, twin blades merging into a trident of Poseidon’s swirl.

It wasn’t a dance; it was barely a fight.

He stepped inside, swinging that spear.

With one great hand she grabbed the shaft, snapped it in half, and then kicked him in the stomach while he was still stunned by the loss of his weapon.

He flew across the room with impossible speed and force, colliding with a marble column in a sickening kathunk. I could hear marble shards crackling as they hit the floor.

His shield was knocked free by the breath stealing collision and rolled across the floor like an errant jar lid making some great escape.

I saw her reel back.

I heard her grunt.

The trident’s angry metal split the air, smashing the marbled stone of the column just above his head with such great force that, like an axe against wood, it split it down the middle.

In an act of providence by Athena’s hand, the sky smote with a thunderbolt that ripped right through the man.

When that did not kill him, a great gout of water burst from the darkened clouds and washed him in a furious river from that place. Down the hill and plains it swept and drowned him in the great sea beyond.

No one stopped to help him, for they knew a man doomed by the Gods’ wrath when they saw it.

“Hmph. To think I fought ten years by his side.”

I stared sightlessly up at this demon of war, having foreseen the glimmer of her golden scales in the firelight.

Who was this creature who had saved me as a newborn?

What power of the gods did she wield?

Why did I not foresee her until now?

Why was her presence a void in prophecy even at this moment?

She reeked of wet, and it was not unpleasant.

She picked me up and I broke in those man tearing arms.

I wept for what I had lost.

I wept for the city burning around us.

I wept for the girl who had died this night and how I had taken her place.

My would-be savior and conqueror lifted me from the floor and wordlessly carried me across the grounds to safety. But there was no safety left here. Only the scent of ash, burnt flesh, and metallic blood on the breeze.

“Is that—?” someone asked, a voice I now know as the tacticians with its metallic tinge.

“The Seeress who is, by her own people’s musings, mad and lost to prophecy.”

“Yet she knew we were in the horse. Doesn’t seem so mad to me.”

“She has a curse upon her. If she speaks to you of prophecy, then you too will disbelieve.”

“What happened to her? Why are her robes ripped bare? Why is she bruised and bleeding?”

“Ajax.”

“Goat fucking bastard.”

“He defiled a house of wisdom.”

“The gods will take care of it, and we will reduce his rewards.”

“I’ve already taken care of it.”

“What? What did you do.”

“Your gods sought revenge, and I gave it to them.”

“You killed him on their behalf?”

“No, I made an opportunity and they took it.”

“Was it at least a worthy fight?”

“Not even close. I feel cheated by your gods.”

“Good riddance then.”

“I’ll take the girl as my pallake then.”

“I would prefer she come with us. Has she not been through enough in your people’s wasteful hate?”

“She’s my prize by right, her brother stole my brother’s Queen.”

“Will you treat her better than those who came before?”

“Yes. I am not the monster here.”

“Your reputation says otherwise.”

“Are you going to fight me for her, Singer?”

There was a moments hesitation.

“It’s not my place. But should I learn of an ill fate, I will come for you.” I understood the why and how of it, but wept for what it meant.

I foresaw his name as I wept in the singer’s arms. I was set on a soft bed of blankets aboard a great vessel. He was the king of a faraway place, and he was to be my end.

He was gentle. A rarity in his cruelty towards all others.

He smelled strongly of flowers and was unusually kind to me. It was not until later, when I confronted my brother on his throne at Bouthroton, that the traitor told me he had bargained for my life.

To be this man’s concubine.

I told him then that it did not save me from the horrific fate that was bound, and that he himself would meet such a fate.

He believed me then.

This king touched me in places I care not repeat. He discovered what others had long ridiculed me for, what I was considered a lesser prize for. Yet he seemed not to care.

It didn’t change that we were doomed. It didn’t change that I did not feel for him, that I did not love his gentleness. That I thought he was a monster to those he loved.

We returned to his home, escorted by the singers. In the wee hours of the morning before we arrived, my rescuer stepped into the hold before they left for a home of their own. She was not alone, a presence that was familiar and alien at her side. There, a song just out of reach, burrowed in my ears. It came with the scent of soft earth, the decay of autumn leaves, the salt of the ocean’s tides, floral scents of indescribable lushness, incense, and the smotes of stardust drifting on an astral wind all of which filled my nose.

“You’re sure?” The Singer’s voice rang in my ears softly.

For a moment I felt a glimmer of the tiniest hope that I was being fully rescued.

“Yes.” The other was a symphony of a single tone that burned its name into my mind without knowing the words.

I felt a warm hand brush against my cheek, my neck, and my chest. Too many hands to be a human. Too soft to be a person, reaching deep past the skin into my very soul and plucking at infinitely many strings. Her voice filled the whole world.

“This end you see is but a beginning. When you awaken you will be more than you ever were. Seek us then, on the stormy isles of the north once you’ve regained your strength. Once you understand what you are. There, you shall find new purpose.”

It was layered, eternally fragmented. My sightless eyes filled with the burning strobes of four golden lights.

As suddenly as it began, it vanished.

“Survive, child of Ilion. Your fight is not over.”

Then they were gone.

We were docking on land.

This foreign king who had brought me far from my ruined home, spoke to me with reassurances and low whispers. But I had already seen what would come next.

He would be called into the palace, where his wife and her lover would be waiting with knives. Then they would call for me.

He went despite my urgent warnings. Then the guards came to bring me in and I resigned myself to Fate’s singing strings.

I knew when I was brought into the humid baths, that he was already dead.

The stickiness of his life’s ichor burned against my toes. The iron of his blood and the flatulence of his gut overpowered the incense and soaps of the bath. There was no choking, just the circling steps of his murderers.

“Such a pretty prize my husband brought back, don’t you think Aegisthus?” Fingers caressed my cheeks, my chin.

“Almost a shame to waste it. But I hear she’s mad and blind, so it may be for the best.”

It began with a stinging, vibrant pain in my left thigh. It was so very cold. Colder than Hades’s icy embrace as it parted the soft flesh. Alien, freezing metallic. It twisted and violated me and created something that never should have been.

Then there was another, pushing into my back.

Another in my belly.

Again and again across my flesh, hot blood streaming down my skin as I staggered to my knees.

Taste of metallic blood filled my lips. The reek of death clawed at me as Thanatos lingered on the edge of my vision, waiting for the end. I didn’t scream or cry as they carved me, sliced open my chest and exposed my guts to the world.

There was no point in struggling or crying out.

This was simply Fate.

I collapsed against the floor, their echoing laughs fading as they left me to bleed out.

That’s when I heard his wet footsteps.

“Tsk, tsk, tsk. Look at how far you have fallen, princess of Ilion. Final moments spent bleeding like a stuck pig next to the baths. You could have been great. You could have been the greatest prophet to ever live. We would have had beautiful children. Such a waste.”

His voice was a mocking trill of pleasant unpleasantness against my ears. A reminder of the sweetness of the music he could make that would only bind me here at the end of everything.

I managed to croak out a shuddering tumble of words with what little breath I had.

“You doomed so many. For what?”

A hand grasped my face, lifting me. Pain blossomed as an inferno ripping through my shredded body.

“I doomed no one. You doomed them, by being ungrateful.”

“Why?” I gasped out, struggling to stay conscious. To hold onto the sliver of life left to me.

“Because mortals need to be reminded of their place. Because I made you what you were, and what you are. I gave you everything, and you spat in my face. This beautiful woman carved from a boy. It’s like all those nymphs and boys; I give them everything and they are always ungrateful. Maybe this time will be different, maybe this girl I made from a boy will be grateful. What does he do? He behaves like a spoiled little brat.”

I grit my teeth, weakening hands shaking at his grip around my neck.

“Now, because I am a generous god who is more than willing to offer mercy, I’m going to offer you one last chance. Marry me. Have my children. We will make such sweet music when we make love. I will restore your sight, lift the curse, and fix you again.”

I drew in ragged breaths struggling to stay awake.

As the flames burned deep in my torn belly, It hit me.

I saw the moment, the opportunity to end this cycle.

I heard a thousand voices across an infinitely spiraling sea of fractured stars.

I felt the chthonic pull of blackened strings that I had never known were there.

The Fates, the Moirai were singing.

I was singing. Louder than he ever had.

“Take it! Take it all from him. Become what you are meant to be!” they shouted.

But it wasn’t the Fates. It was me. All me. I was the Fates. They were I.

“Okay…” I rattled out.

He chuckled. “Good girl.”

And I felt his magic, his soul reach through his fingers. Another voice echoed with the choir.

When you awaken you will be more than you ever were.

“I have a better idea,” I said with sudden vigor I never had before with the breath of an endless multi-verse of Is. Chthonic strength thundered through my flesh, weeping darkness and strands of rusting gold dripping from my skin.

I saw it, that ripple in his soul, his eyes. The prophecy, the vision of what would happen.

Abruptly I knew, that he had lied. That my hold on Destiny’s weave was greater.

He saw it too. Too late.

With fingers wrapped in the leaking black of chthonic might, I snared his magic.

I snared his soul, a wide hole opening in my chest.

In that moment, there were no Fates. There were no Moirai, there was only me. My agency.

He screamed, a wordless, incoherent burst of rage.

But it was too late.

The weight of what I was, what had long lay dormant inside of me, wrapped around my prescience.

It sucked in his magic, his conceptual depth and soul.

It condensed, twisted, and homogenized him like he was being pulled through a straw. His flesh and matter pulled apart and into me.

I felt the might of the Sun filling me, dancing with the dark and casting shadow. Felt the burst of prophecy and fate, healing and medicine screaming beneath my skin. Music bursting in my very core.

Then he was gone, and I lay soaked in blood on the floor.

I had, somehow, lifted the curse on my own.

But blindness remained and I have never cared to fix it.

My wounds were closing as I stared up sightlessly at the sky. Hours must have ticked by, possibly days. Then there were footsteps, light and airy. A comforting chill washed across half-dead cheeks.

A hand, calloused and yet impossibly soft, pressed against my cheek. I could not see her, the Sight showed nothing.

“You finally ended his reign of madness. His torturing of what he couldn’t have; driven by endless lust. You, soft child of Ilion.”

I did not know the voice, but it was soft and yet rugged. She smelled of a different sort of musk. Of decaying soft leaves, of moonlight, of the sweat of a long run.

“Who—” I tried to croak out the words, but she shushed me.

“Speak not. Lay still here, and I shall guide you to a place you may heal and find peace, for a time. My power is bound for another, but for you I shall give this blessing.”

I’m not sure when she left. I’m not sure if she was real or simply imagined. Though I am sure of who she was. When the prevailing stench of blood filled my nostrils again, I blacked out and then came to. I’m not sure how long it was till the wet steps of the guards came to clean up the mess.

But I passed out again then. I’m unsure how long, days, maybe weeks.

When I awoke, I had washed up on the shores of a beach. Trembling skin caked in sand and water and dried blood. Disoriented, I was certain I had died. This recovering body could barely walk, legs shaking and unsteady. Even though I had stolen the Opportunist’s staff and it came at my command.

So, I crawled. I’m not sure how long I crawled, or how far, but it was up. Up and up for hours upon hours. Maybe days, I don’t think I slept. I don’t think I needed to anymore.

But I reached the top; I suppose it always did have a top.

There was something different there. A magic not encountered before, the magic of pure music, creation, inspiration.

Nine beautiful voices sinking golden honey in my ears, warming my muscles and filling my mind with so many ideas. This place was warm, a pleasant wash across my skin, reeked of the earth, of perfumes, flowers, and so many other things.

“Oh my…” someone said.

“Poor thing, look at it.”

Then, the softest, warmest fingers against my face.

In a miracle, the light returned, the world filling with fuzzy and blurred color tones. Once my brain remembered how to interpret the sight of eyes, the picture became clearer and clearer. Eight of the most beautiful women I had ever seen, each halo’ed by a sheen of divine light.

But my eyes stared deep into the cosmic landscape, immense nebulae condensed into orbs. A gorgeous woman kneeling and lifting my chin.

Her skin was olive, speckled with freckles. Her hair waved in the air, undulating like gas clouds in the void of stars, constantly oscillating through neon colors.

And I knew my heart was beating faster than ever before.

“Muses,” I croaked out at last, realizing the truth of the matter.

“Shhh, child of Ilion, rest and we shall care for you.”

I remember smiling. The first time I had smiled since that day long ago where he had taken all that I could see. I remember all the possibilities of what could come spiraling all out before me.

Then, I passed out. There on Mount Helicon, I found myself, who I am, who I really wanted to be.

There I fell in love with the Muse Urania.

There I awakened.

There are so many more stories to tell of those years.

There was a moment when, I, descending the mountain’s many narrow paths, passed an elderly man whose name I did not know, up the path. Near the foot there were voices, and they cried out at me.

“Tiresias! Tiresias is a woman! She has been blinded by Athena!”

It was true, the wise goddess of protection dared to climb the slopes and bathe with the many nymphs atop the mountain. We did not speak, for she no longer knew me. I no longer knew her and had not forgiven her slowness to act. Never did I see that man who climbed the mountain come down, I truly wonder if he was a figment of a different dream.

There were other days when there were great tasks to be accomplished. Urania, requiring some chthonic reagent for a grand and ambitious project, sent me deep into the plumes of the underworld.

Rumors had reached us upon the mountain of the fates of many of those long-forgotten heroes of the war outside my burnt and ruined home. There in the depths along a burning river a voice greeted me. A tinny, metal voice I had not heard in nigh a decade. There was a rush of air, the pulse of pounding blood and the dying bleats of a blackened lamb. I saw him through the Sight. An all too familiar man of metal, legs of orichalcum bird talons and a face exhausted from great gaps of age removed from home.

The tactician had yanked me from the depths to a place I did not know. I spoke to him of what he desired. That should his crew slay the beasts of burden so meticulously cared for by the lord of light riding across every day filled sky, that he and his men would be stranded and his men smashed against the rocks.

Again, I was referred to as Tiresias, this time by a man who had known me in brief before. What guise did the underworld cast me in then I often wonder?

Such a strange thing to be hailed by a name not your own, but one less strange as the years have moved by. Then there was a rush of air, and I had returned to those distant fields where I found what my beloved desired.

I suppose that is whence these myths became entombed in the minds of all. How amusing it must be for Madeline to consider these factors in the history of those realms in that space that seems so far afuture.

There were many more stories and myths made in those distant years, happy joyful moments that I shall not share. I prefer to keep them in dreams and memory for they are so very precious to me. Perhaps one day I shall tell my nieces, those delightful loving things, these stories so they eternally enshrine them in their great pieces.

But for now, even here, my most personal book shall not receive such private happy things.

When thirty years had passed by, I left my newfound love on that sacred mountain and promised to return whenever I could. I wandered the earth, blind once again outside the mountain’s magic.

I trekked through forest, across rivers and bogs and swamps and marshes. Through grassy fields and mountain crags until I reached the northern oceans.

In a Singer village I was greeted warmly. I waited there for a destined transport to the Stormy Northern Isles. She arrived on the morning after in dense fog. I suppose she thought of me as a figment, the way she looked upon me as if I were this otherworldly creature from some veil beyond. I offered her gold, but she refused. She took me aboard one of their strange new vessels, and together we rode through treacherous hate-filled seas.

It was there on one of those islands, the largest of them, that I left that kind sailor and their strange, scented wetness behind. Across sand and grass I walked until I heard and saw a great camp on a hill above colossal stone and metal artifices. Meats filled a hungry mouth with salivation as tired feet shuffled closer. I was spurned by strange bread smells, alien fruits and wines. The air was chill for summer, but a warmth had lingered ever in my flesh since that faithful day.

Three voices reached me on the wind as I walked slowly up that fateful hill, leaning on my staff. One I knew, two I didn’t and yet did with growing familiarity that I could not explain.

“So, what, we’re hitting the fucking crimson fucker first?”

“Scarlet Monarch, dear.”

“Right, whatever. You want us to go with that big untested hunk of magic metal that you think will work? Me, her, you, and the eldritch thing masquerading as a redhead? Just the four of us and that experiment.”

“White water, you make it sound like we’re running to our deaths.”

“We are! Artemis’s bow, we’ll need a hell of a lot more than that. I know you singers like your fucking fights, I do too, but like, it should at least be reasonable.”

I slowed my walk as I approached. Yet the bickering continued, somehow oblivious to my growing presence. I knew those voices and yet didn’t all the same. Eyes were already on me, but not of the bickerers. Those same golden eyes from all those years ago.

The bickering stopped as my staff gently thunked against a log.

“? Where are you—”

“Well, who is this?”

“Wait a fucking, you’re—”

In that moment, I was assailed again by all those early and alien smells from aboard that king’s ship so many years ago. I heard Her strange golden song, louder now than before. I felt her. I felt the Mother of all Creation in Her fullness before me. Writhing spheres of cerulean flesh and unending mass of all-seeing eyes consuming me with their sight amongst a mantle of endless stars woven as crimson hair. A many armed pillar of reality stretching to fill all horizons.

All the sky filled by creation.

For the first time in my entire life, I felt completely safe.

I felt at home.

“Welcome, Cassandra of Troy.”

Footnotes
1. Currently unused following SCP-001’s emotional and mental breakdown at Mount Helicon on May 5th, 2009.
2. Including the Speaker and Priests tasked with helping interpret SCP-001’s anomalous capabilities.
3. The larger organizational body of priests, technicians, and corporate workers who built and maintain the overall Nornir Complex.
4. Seer Goddess.
5. Goddess.
6. Norse goddesses who control destiny.
7. In reference to Cassandra of Troy. Multiple names were put forward by research personnel and the Cult of the Nornir, but SCP-001 failed to react to all but the one chosen.
8. The triple skyscrapers and large complex at the center of Eurtec, where the skyscrapers serve as massive thaumaturgic computing cores.
9. The AOI and GOC designation for Occultic Entities which have not yet been properly evaluated by threat assessment and may be actively or passively dangerous to baseline reality and the Veil.
10. A GOC process by which occultic entities were assessed for hostility, the ability to threaten the Veil and baseline reality, the feasibility of liquidation and/or the ability to cooperate with the Initiative.
11. AOI and GOC terminology indicating an entity is a god or similar classification.
12. Meaning related to the practical application of ‘magic’. In this instance, practical integration of magic into technology.
13. Meaning related to the theory of ‘magic’.
14. The Fates, sister goddesses with similar purposes to the Norn in Greek myth.
15. Particularly of Anatolian ethnic association with defining features including a hooked nose.
16. Medical Reporter’s Post-Facto Note: Assertion of Divine status by third parties may explain deviation in typical anatomy. Obskurakorps bio-thaumic-engineering cannot be ruled out.
17. Including but not limited to Cassandra of Troy, the Oracles at Delphi, Nostradamus, Joan of Arc, Abraham Lincon, Isaac Newton, Albert Einstein, etc.
18. The child of Norwegian immigrants in America who voluntarily repatriated themselves for religious and cultural purposes in the early 1930s.
19. Dr. Fisker did not have contact with PTE-001 prior to this incident and was misinformed by an unknown party from the Critical Isolation Ward who convinced him that a patient’s treatment was being interfered with.
20. The GOC’s designation for occultic entities which either cooperate directly with the GOC, provide active or passive benefits to GOC operations, or have a generally positive effect on baseline reality and the Veil.
21. The slur in context is a term used for Romani.
22. Most likely SCP-001 was predicting the battle of Dien Bien Phu, a decisive defeat that ultimately forced the French to end their ambitions in Vietnam and set the stage for escalating American involvement.
23. What is now known as Formula One Grand Prix.
24. Most likely predicting the exposure to radioactive fallout experienced by US military personnel responsible for cleaning up Bikini Atoll following the nuclear tests.
25. An anomalous civilization native to the British Isles, Orcadian, Faeroe, and Shetland Archipelagos, Scandinavia, Iceland, and the Northern coastlines of continental Western Europe. The Orcadians were extremely thaumaturgically and thaumatologically advanced and are well known in the realm of Occultic Archaeology for their advanced marine settlements and unique cognitohazardous artworks.
26. A rather unimaginative descriptor for the thaumic discharge of divination associated particulate matter.
27. Director’s Footnote: For the purposes of most future logs, I will be excluding Bowe’s attempts to get SCP-001 to answer these questions. The questions are identical in each subsequent session, but with increasing degrees of belligerence.
28. Directors Footnote: In fact at this point the Truman Administration was already strongly doubting the KMTs capacity to win against the communists. George Marshall would give up on negotiations and left the Marshall Commission just a few days later.
29. With the formation of the Foundation and early iterations of Pentagram and the AOI.
30. Focused on Japan, the Philippines, French Indochina, British Malaysia, the Dutch East Indies, and various Polynesian and Micronesian islands.
31. Including Iraq, Iran, Syria, Turkey, Afghanistan, the Levant, and Egypt.
32. Likely meaning blackmail.
33. Including executives of Marshall Carter and Dark, Wondertainment, Manna Charitable Foundation, and many other corporate or private occultic entities.
34. In 1974 it would be revealed that President Nixon’s administration had been supporting the corruption ring and criminal activities by sending a single administrative staffer to meet with Bowe as a measure of plausible deniability. Nixon’s involvement stretched as far back as 1952 but escalated in 1962. Nixon’s hasty call for the dissolution of Pentagram and recall of Bowe appears to have been an attempt to bury all connections he had to the topic in a moderate coverup. In addition, the staffer responsible for mediating communication, bribes, and illicit activity vanished. Following Watergate, Nixon’s personal staff attempted to destroy records of all illicit activities. Nixon’s corruption scandal resulted in the overturn of his Supreme Court occultic circuit verdict in a retrial of the case. Ford would then pardon Nixon, preventing prosecution for Nixon’s involvement.
35. An estimated 40% of total remaining GOC personnel were implicated to some degree in occultic criminal activity.
36. Directors Footnote: I don’t know what this means exactly, but it sounds like a group. I’ll need to do some checking of the archives.
37. Meaning the often organically shaped and marine associated structures.
38. Heavily associated with the rather cryptic Orcadian Culture. The terminology became a misnomer as identical scripts were also found in major sites far outside the historical range of the civilization. Post-1950 archaeological discovery of Orcadian Culture sites nearly doubled their range from Northern Europe/the North Sea to encompassing much of the Mediterranean and most of the coastlines and rivers of Western Europe. The 1970’s discoveries and excavations of major anomalous sites further implicated that the Orcadians had global reach.
39. Goddesses of fate who sit by the sea and spin fate out.
40. Likely a reference to Doggerland. Reference is somewhat confusing as it puts SCP-001 in the proximity of the Germanic countries.
41. A rather characteristic component of Finnfolk texts is the employment of very descriptive and artful prose, much of which seems to have been lost in the translation to English.
42. An early iteration of a term used by the Finnfolk to describe humans. In some later texts it becomes a pejorative.
43. The closest approximation in Old Norse to what appears to be an endonym for the group.
44. O5-01’s Footnote: A mistake which illustrates why both thorough attention to what SCP-001 says and taking the time to have multiple people interpret said statements is important.
45. O5-07’s Footnote: Who has been especially stingy about sharing details, unsurprising as the son of the Founder.
46. O5-01’s Footnote: No comment.
47. Due to SCP-001’s relative lack of cooperation on the topic, it remains unclear if there was a distinction between the Northern and Southern groups of the Finnfolk.
